candidate_problems.text: Can the conjecture be made mathematically nontrivial and testable by defining a decomposition-loss metric that uniquely picks out 'globally necessary but locally underprovided' variables, rather than merely redescribing ordinary emergence, synergy, or model-selection tradeoffs? The open question is what formal criterion distinguishes perspectival underprovision from existing notions in information theory, control, and multiscale modeling, and whether that criterion yields clear positive and negative cases.

Is the proposed public-goods structure actually explanatory of consciousness, or only of why third-person scientific models systematically omit certain global variables? The unresolved tension is between an epistemic thesis about failures of decomposition in explanation and an ontological thesis about consciousness itself: why should a variable being non-locally attributable in our best models imply that the system has genuine first-person organization, rather than just that our explanatory practices have coordination costs?

What empirical signature would separate conscious systems from merely highly integrated nonconscious systems under this framework? The conjecture claims that some architectures require an observer-indexed global state to predict/report/control unified discriminations, but it remains open how to operationalize this across brains, artificial systems, split-brain cases, anesthesia, blindsight, recurrent networks, or distributed control systems without collapsing into circularity or over-ascribing consciousness.
